branch_profiles.tagline: Software, algorithms, and systems -- the most in-demand branch, also the most crowded.
suits_students_who: Actually enjoy debugging -- not just the idea of coding, the slow grind of fixing broken things
Are comfortable with abstract, symbolic problem-solving rather than physical/hands-on work
Don't mind that the field changes every few years and expects continuous self-learning
less_good_fit_if: You're choosing CSE mainly because 'it has the best placements' without any real curiosity about programming itself
You strongly prefer hands-on, physical, or visual work over screen-based abstract work
You're hoping for a fixed, stable curriculum that won't keep changing -- it will
further_study_paths: Direct industry entry is the most common path for this branch specifically
branch_profiles.data_disclaimer: No placement or salary figures are claimed here. Those numbers vary a lot by college and year -- ask your specific college's placement cell for real, current data rather than relying on internet averages.

branch_profiles.tagline: Very close to CSE in practice, with more weight on networks, web systems, and enterprise software.
suits_students_who: Want the CSE-adjacent career path but at a college/cutoff where CSE itself wasn't an option
Lean slightly more toward systems, networks, and applied software over deep algorithmic theory
less_good_fit_if: You specifically want the heaviest theoretical CS depth (algorithms, theory of computation) -- CSE leans further that direction
career_paths: IT services and enterprise software roles
branch_profiles.data_disclaimer: No placement or salary figures are claimed here. In practice, recruiters increasingly treat CSE and IT similarly, but this varies by company -- check specific companies' eligibility criteria if this matters to you.

branch_profiles.tagline: The hardware-software bridge: circuits, signals, communication systems, and embedded devices.
suits_students_who: Like both physics/circuits and programming, and don't want to fully commit to one or the other
Are curious how physical devices (phones, chips, sensors) actually work underneath the software
Are fine with a heavier, more varied course load than pure CSE
less_good_fit_if: You actively dislike circuit theory and signal processing math -- it's a large, unavoidable part of the curriculum
You want a branch with one obvious, single career direction -- ECE careers fork in several directions and that ambiguity itself can be stressful
career_paths: Many ECE graduates also move into software roles, since the branch builds strong general problem-solving
further_study_paths: GATE -- a particularly common and well-trodden path for ECE
branch_profiles.data_disclaimer: No placement or salary figures are claimed here.

branch_profiles.tagline: Power systems, electrical machines, and control -- the branch behind the grid, not the gadget.
suits_students_who: Are genuinely interested in power generation, transmission, and the energy sector -- including renewables
Want a path toward government/PSU electrical roles (state electricity boards, NTPC-type organizations)
Don't mind a branch with less buzz than CSE but real, stable core-industry demand
less_good_fit_if: Your real interest is software and you're choosing EEE only as a fallback -- the curriculum won't scratch that itch
You want fast-moving, trend-driven work -- power systems engineering moves slowly and deliberately by design
career_paths: Some graduates pivot into software/analytics roles
further_study_paths: GATE -- strong PSU recruitment route specifically for EEE
MBA, for those moving toward energy-sector management
branch_profiles.data_disclaimer: No placement or salary figures are claimed here.

branch_profiles.tagline: Structures, construction, and infrastructure -- the most tangible, real-world branch of the six.
suits_students_who: Want to see physical, tangible results of their work -- buildings, roads, bridges
Are drawn to government infrastructure roles (PWD, municipal engineering, railways) or construction-industry careers
Are comfortable with significant site/field work, not just office or lab work
less_good_fit_if: You want a desk-based, software-centric daily routine -- site visits and field components are a real part of this branch
Your interest is purely in the design/software side (e.g. architecture/CAD) without the structural engineering fundamentals underneath it
further_study_paths: GATE -- well-established route into government infrastructure jobs and PSUs
branch_profiles.data_disclaimer: No placement or salary figures are claimed here.

branch_profiles.tagline: Machines, manufacturing, and thermal systems -- the broadest, most classically 'engineering' branch.
suits_students_who: Like understanding how physical machines and systems actually work, from engines to manufacturing lines
Are interested in the automotive, manufacturing, or industrial/production sectors
Want one of the broadest, most flexible engineering foundations -- mechanical concepts show up almost everywhere
less_good_fit_if: You're specifically chasing software-only career paths -- mechanical's curriculum is mostly hardware/physical-systems focused
career_paths: Many graduates also move into software, analytics, or further study
further_study_paths: MBA -- common route for those moving toward operations/management
branch_profiles.data_disclaimer: No placement or salary figures are claimed here.
